chunk_summary: Guidelines for prescribing post-discharge formulas for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants, emphasizing stepwise escalation, nutrient-enriched formulas, and safety considerations for hydration and preparation practices.
learning_objectives: Understand the caloric and nutrient differences between standard, higher-calorie, and post-discharge preterm formulas.
Learn the stepwise escalation strategy for addressing poor growth in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge.
Recognize safety concerns, including hydration, renal solute load, and formula preparation for high-risk infants.
key_facts: Standard term formula provides 19–20 kcal/oz (~0.67 kcal/mL).
Higher-calorie term formula (22–24 kcal/oz) is used when infants cannot consume sufficient volume.
Post-discharge preterm formula is nutrient-enriched, often 22 kcal/oz, with higher protein and minerals.
Stage 1: Optimize volume and frequency; Stage 2: Targeted enrichment; Stage 3: Reassess within 5–7 days.
Concentrations above 24 kcal/oz can reduce free water and increase renal solute load; monitor hydration and electrolytes.
Powdered formula is not sterile; high-risk infants require careful preparation and storage.
reference_quote: Post-discharge preterm formula is nutrient-enriched (often 22 kcal/oz with higher protein and minerals than term formula) and can be used as a supplement or primary feed when growth is poor. (Source: American Academy of Pediatrics)
document_key_topics: Post-discharge formula options for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants
Safety considerations in formula preparation and hydration
